query: html parsing and rendering interview question

competitors.label: How browsers work
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN performance guide for parsing, DOM and CSSOM construction, render-tree work, and the blocking points that affect what users see.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the browser rendering pipeline and on how HTML, CSS, and scripts interact during parsing and paint.
theirGaps: The guide is broader and less interview-shaped than a compact answer focused on render-blocking CSS, synchronous scripts, and common parse-order debugging failures.

realWorldUseCases: Browsers parse HTML progressively, build the DOM and CSSOM, then layout and paint what users see. Under the hood, synchronous scripts and render-blocking CSS can pause this pipeline and create hard-to-debug performance issues.
followUpConfusion: Browsers do not wait for the full HTML file before starting work. They parse the document progressively, build the DOM, parse CSS into the CSSOM, then combine both into render data for layout and paint.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the pipeline comprehensively, but it is lighter on the exact production debugging hooks this question usually targets: render-blocking CSS, parser-blocking scripts, and DOM-ready timing mistakes.
actionableExamples: It includes strong pipeline diagrams and examples, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the next confusion developers hit in practice: parsing is progressive, CSS blocks rendering, and synchronous scripts can pause the parser outright.

actionableExamples: Keep the short pipeline timeline visible so the page stays faster to scan than a long browser-architecture guide.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical browser-pipeline guide, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it compresses parsing and rendering into the exact blocking and timing failures developers debug in real apps.
